segments.title: A battlecruiser reserve at Singapore
segments.summary: The campaign review finds four battlecruisers and two light cruisers at Singapore, illustrating how a distant squadron can become an operational reserve.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke takes stock of heavy ships already based at Singapore while deciding where to move forces. Four battlecruisers give the station substantial striking power, while two light cruisers add scouting and screening capacity. Their presence also means that a distant base can supply a ready reserve without waiting for ships to arrive from home waters. The moment is useful for seeing forward basing as a force-structure choice rather than simply a point on the map.

evidence.note: He identifies the ships at Singapore as four battlecruisers and two light cruisers.

segments.title: Crew capacity before expanding the submarine force
segments.summary: Maximum crew training and transport capacity underpin a decision to expand the submarine arm rather than treating new hulls as cost-free additions.
segments.body: The campaign screen makes personnel generation, training, transport capacity, research funding, and shipbuilding compete in the same budgetary picture. Crew training and transport capacity are both set to maximum while the navy still earns money, giving Dr. Clarke room to expand. He then orders sixteen coastal submarines rather than treating new hulls as cost-free additions. The sequence shows why a navy's manpower and support capacity matter alongside the number of ships it can finance.

evidence.note: He reviews maximum crew training and transport capacity before ordering coastal submarines.

segments.title: Blockading France as economic warfare
segments.summary: A fleet concentrated off France is expected to damage the French economy, linking sea control to pressure on an enemy's ability to sustain war.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke checks whether the long-running blockade is finally tormenting the French economy. He points to the quantity of British shipping stationed off France and expects that pressure to make normal economic activity increasingly difficult. The campaign mechanic presents blockade as an effect of sustained naval presence rather than a single battle. This watch point connects fleet concentration with economic warfare and the strategic purpose of sea control.

evidence.note: He describes the ships off France as a blockade intended to cripple the French economy.

segments.title: Screening and scouting ahead of the battle line
segments.summary: Light cruisers and destroyers are assigned to screen, scout, and hunt while the capital ships form the battle line behind them.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explicitly sets a light cruiser to screen and scout for the battle line, then detaches other ships to search. The heavier ships remain the centre around which those light forces operate. The resulting task force of a capital ship, three light cruisers, and roughly nine destroyers demonstrates the layered jobs of scouts, escorts, and striking units. It also shows how formation orders turn a collection of hulls into a force with distinct tactical roles.

evidence.note: He assigns screening and scouting orders and describes the detached task force.

segments.title: Why fleet speed has to match the force
segments.summary: A fast battlecruiser is grouped with ships limited to 24 knots, highlighting the practical cost of mixing unequal speeds in one formation.
segments.body: The commentary compares a 30-knot ship with older battlecruisers that are now roughly as fast as newer battleships. Dr. Clarke deliberately assigns the fast ship to a formation limited to 24 knots. A task force is constrained by the ships it must keep together, so the fastest hull does not set the useful speed by itself. Speed matching therefore affects whether a mobile unit can exploit its design advantage or must slow to the formation's pace.

evidence.note: He compares the speed of Conqueror and older battlecruisers with the formation's 24-knot limit.

segments.title: Secondary batteries create a crossfire problem
segments.summary: A French battlecruiser is caught under concentrated six-inch fire, showing how numerous secondary guns can punish a lighter opponent at close range.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that the opposing ship has fifteen 15-inch guns but faces a battlecruiser with a dense secondary battery as well as heavy guns. Multiple British ships bring six-inch weapons to bear from different directions. That concentration makes the engagement dangerous even before the heaviest shells decide it. The encounter stresses that a broadside is more than its biggest calibre: secondary guns and crossfire geometry can shape a close-range capital-ship fight.

evidence.note: He describes the six-inch crossfire and compares the opposing heavy-gun batteries.

segments.title: Range, speed, and armor in a battlecruiser duel
segments.summary: The engagement weighs gun range, best-speed accuracy, ammunition, and armor before deciding how much risk is acceptable against a tougher opponent.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke holds his ship near the edge of its 21-kilometre gun range and keeps 25 knots as the preferred speed for accuracy. He argues against closing with a battleship whose range and armour are at least as good. When six-inch ammunition runs out and the enemy's thick protection continues to absorb punishment, pressing the attack becomes a greater gamble. The sequence is a compact illustration of how range, speed, protection, and ammunition impose limits on tactical freedom.

evidence.note: He compares gun ranges and speed, then discusses armor, ammunition, and the risks of continuing the fight.

segments.title: Why a distant-station cruiser needs torpedoes
segments.summary: A cruiser operating away from battleship support is judged to need torpedoes as protection and a means of creating a close-range threat.
segments.body: Watching an enemy cruiser stand off at range, Dr. Clarke says he would instead close for torpedo attacks if the ship carried them. Remaining at gun range leaves the cruiser exposed to a stronger opponent without creating a comparable threat. His point is about the vulnerability of cruisers on distant stations, away from battleship cover. A torpedo armament gives a lighter vessel a way to complicate an enemy's choices and may force the heavier ship to manoeuvre defensively.

evidence.note: He explains why a cruiser away from battleship cover should have torpedoes.

segments.title: Bulkheads improve survival but cost weight
segments.summary: Maximum reinforced bulkheads help contain flooding and fire, but their weight reduces the margin available for fuel and other design choices.
segments.body: The design discussion treats internal subdivision as a genuine survivability benefit rather than a free upgrade. Dr. Clarke prefers maximum reinforced bulkheads because they make a large difference to containing fire and flooding. The protection is not free: it adds hull weight that must be recovered elsewhere in the design. Fuel capacity and endurance are among the competing requirements, making subdivision part of the ship's overall weight bargain.

evidence.note: He describes reinforced bulkheads as valuable for fire and flooding control but costly in hull weight.

segments.title: Concentrating fleets for an invasion of northern France
segments.summary: The campaign shifts fleets toward northern France, illustrating how an invasion plan absorbs available ships, bases, crews, and older hulls.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens another naval-invasion option and repeatedly moves ships north to improve the chance of success. He draws forces back from other stations, reviews ships in repair, and commissions available units for the operation. Older vessels are scrapped while usable hulls are concentrated in the required theatre. The watch point shows how amphibious pressure depends on assembling enough operational force in the right place, not simply possessing high total tonnage somewhere on the map.

evidence.note: He plans invasions and moves fleets and available ships toward northern France.

segments.title: Fleet renewal depends on the crew pool
segments.summary: New ships enter service while older hulls are scrapped so scarce trained crews can move into the modern fleet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke reviews an expanding fleet while several newly completed ships remain mothballed for lack of crews. He sorts the fleet by age and begins scrapping vessels dating from 1911 onward rather than keeping every hull in commission. A large light-cruiser building programme means the navy can retire older examples without losing that capability. The sequence shows fleet renewal as a personnel-allocation problem: building a replacement does not make it operational until trained sailors can be transferred to it.

evidence.note: He scraps older ships and explains that new ships are mothballed until crews become available.

segments.title: A capital ship designed to escort itself
segments.summary: The design idea is a powerful ship whose six-inch battery can handle destroyers and light cruisers, reducing its dependence on close escort support.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke considers whether a sufficiently powerful ship can help protect itself from smaller cruisers and destroyers. A dense six-inch battery has already proved effective against light opponents in the campaign, encouraging him to build that capability into a capital ship. The idea does not remove the need for scouting or formation support, but it could make the ship less vulnerable when escorts are absent or distracted. The thought experiment highlights a recurring force-design tradeoff: secondary armament consumes weight and space that could serve speed, armour, range, or a dedicated escort force.

evidence.note: He considers a powerful ship using six-inch guns to deal with destroyers and light cruisers.

segments.title: Balancing a super-battleship design
segments.summary: A super-battleship design is reduced from an extravagant starting point by trading speed, hull protection, armament layout, and weight balance.
segments.body: The design starts at 104,000 tons and is repeatedly reduced from its maximum settings. Speed falls from 33 to 30 knots, hull and torpedo protection are chosen, and pitch, roll, engine efficiency, and weight offsets are monitored. Dr. Clarke compares 16.5-inch and larger guns, moves turrets to improve balance, and preserves heavy belt and deck armour. The completed arrangement demonstrates naval architecture as a sequence of linked compromises rather than a search for the largest possible statistic.

evidence.note: He adjusts speed, protection, armament, stability, and weight while designing a super-battleship.

segments.title: Designing for cruising range and future upgrades
segments.summary: The super-battleship balances 10,900 kilometres of endurance against weight, while an oversized barbette preserves room for a later turret upgrade.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke checks that the design can cover about 10,900 kilometres at a cruising speed of 17.7 knots before returning to armour and armament. That range matters because an ocean-going capital ship must reach distant theatres as well as win a gun comparison. He also retains a larger barbette than the current triple turret requires so a future refit could install a quadruple mount. The watch point links immediate weight control with lifecycle planning, showing how designers may accept present inefficiency to preserve later modernization options.

evidence.note: He checks cruising range and speed, then keeps a large barbette for a possible future quadruple turret.

segments.title: The Hood class: broadside and fire arcs
segments.summary: The completed Hood-class design combines nine 16.5-inch guns with an eighteen-gun six-inch broadside and considers how many secondary guns cover each end of the ship.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke names the new generation of battleship after Hood and inventories its main and secondary batteries. Its broadside combines nine 16.5-inch guns with eighteen six-inch guns. He separately counts the secondary weapons able to fire forward and astern, because not every mounted gun bears on every target angle. The comparison is a reminder that tactical firepower depends on placement and arcs as well as total gun count.

evidence.note: He gives the Hood-class main and secondary armament and describes its forward and stern coverage.

segments.title: Do not open a gap in a convoy screen
segments.summary: A convoy escort that charges out after an attacker can leave the merchant ships exposed, giving a fast surface raider a route through the screen.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke deliberately drives for the merchants and uses the outcome to explain a convoy-protection failure. The defending cruisers charge outward and leave an opening through which the attacker can reach the cargo ships. Escorts must prevent that penetration rather than abandon the vessels they were meant to shield. He also notes that an attacking surface raider must still get clear without taking prohibitive damage.

evidence.note: He explains how an escort charging an attacker can leave a gap that exposes the convoy.

segments.title: Togo faced criticism before Tsushima
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke recalls that Admiral Togo faced pressure before Tsushima because the Russian fleet had not yet come out to be defeated.
segments.body: The short historical aside places Togo's later reputation in a less certain pre-battle context. Dr. Clarke notes that critics viewed the preceding campaign as ineffective while the Russian fleet remained in harbour. Pressure became severe enough that some protests called for Togo's dismissal or suicide. The episode is a useful reminder that famous commanders were not always judged as inevitable victors before their decisive battle.

evidence.note: He says that Togo was criticised before Tsushima because the Russian fleet had not left harbour.

segments.title: Using light cruisers to torpedo a heavy cruiser
segments.summary: A light-cruiser force closes on a heavier cruiser to launch torpedoes before its seven-inch guns can exploit the lighter ships' thin armor.
segments.body: Facing a heavy cruiser, a light cruiser, and destroyers, Dr. Clarke identifies the heavy cruiser as the immediate threat and plans to get torpedoes into it on the first run. Its 7.1-inch guns can seriously damage the light cruisers' 6.6-inch armour if allowed to bear effectively. The attack therefore combines a timed turn to avoid incoming torpedoes with a close approach intended to limit the target's chance to evade. It illustrates how torpedoes can let lighter ships threaten a heavier gun platform, but only if the approach is coordinated and survivable.

evidence.note: He plans a first-run torpedo attack on the heavy cruiser and describes the approach and evasion timing.

segments.title: Merchant ships can still carry a sting
segments.summary: A seemingly undefended merchant convoy reveals torpedo capability, prompting a comparison with armed merchant cruisers.
segments.body: After finding no warships in the convoy, Dr. Clarke discovers that merchant ships can still launch torpedoes. The weapons force an attacker among the merchant columns to keep manoeuvring rather than treating the targets as harmless. He connects the surprise to armed merchant cruisers and argues that torpedoes would have made such vessels much more dangerous historically. The moment is a reminder not to treat merchant traffic as tactically inert simply because it lacks a visible escort.

evidence.note: He discovers that merchant ships have torpedoes and compares the idea with armed merchant cruisers.

segments.title: Two ways to evade a torpedo
segments.summary: A torpedo attack prompts a concise contrast between turning away to outrun it and turning toward it to dodge across its path.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes two basic evasive choices: turn away and attempt to run clear, or turn toward the torpedo and cross its track. Turning away increases the distance the weapon must travel but may keep the ship exposed for longer. Turning toward can reduce the time spent broadside to the attack, though it demands enough room and response time. The right choice depends on relative speed and geometry, making this a useful starting point for reading close-range torpedo manoeuvres.

evidence.note: He contrasts turning away to outrun a torpedo with turning toward it to dodge.

segments.title: More invasion tonnage does not guarantee success
segments.summary: Even 1.5 million tons against an 845,000-ton requirement produces only a 29 percent invasion chance, pointing to enemy ships and submarines as additional constraints.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares the 1.5 million tons assembled for northern France with the operation's stated requirement of 845,000 tons. Despite that numerical superiority, the displayed chance of success remains only 29 percent. He then checks the area and finds French cruisers, destroyers, and submarines opposing the invasion concentration. The sequence shows that amphibious success cannot be reduced to friendly tonnage alone when enemy interference and local operational conditions still matter.

evidence.note: He compares the assembled tonnage and low success chance, then inventories enemy ships and submarines in the area.
